title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: Started from 25 raw findings across four personas and collapsed them to 12. The strongest signal is the unanimous one: all four personas independently flagged `console.log(JSON.stringify(theData))` dumping the raw pre-validation request body — i.e. PAN/CVV — to stdout; kept as a `must` (the perf_skeptic CPU framing was folded in but is secondary to the PCI violation). Three personas agreed on the silently-swallowed coupon catch and on the unknown-coupon debug log (merged the log-injection, debug-leftover, and event-loop framings into one). Two agreed on the always-zero discount_amount bug and on FixedCouponStrategy returning negative charge amounts (kept `must` — it's an exploitable money path). Single-persona but verified-true findings kept: couponCode bypassing the Zod schema, snake_case response fields, `as any`, var/==, poor names, missing tests, and the dead FREESHIP comment. I merged the 'abstract class is really an interface' nit into the broader over-engineering finding (same hierarchy, same code). I pruned the perf_skeptic 'O(payload) CPU' and 'event-loop backpressure / 500 threads' framings as over-broad — the underlying lines are real but the cost story isn't the reason to block; those lines survive under the security/quality findings instead.

findings.title: console.log of raw request body writes plaintext PAN/CVV to logs
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` serialises the entire unvalidated `req.body` — which on a charge endpoint contains card number, CVV, and expiry — to stdout on every request, bypassing the project's pino redaction allowlist. In any environment shipping logs to an aggregator this is a PCI-DSS violation (Req 3.3 prohibits storing CVC anywhere; Req 10.5.1 keeps cardholder data out of logs). Remove the line entirely and rotate any test PANs already logged; if request tracing is needed, log only non-sensitive fields (e.g. customerId) through the structured logger. (Also runs synchronously on the hot path for every charge — secondary, but another reason it shouldn't ship.)

findings.title: discount_amount is always 0 — payload.amount mutated before the subtraction
findings.message: `payload.amount = finalAmount` overwrites the original amount, so `response.discount_amount = payload.amount - finalAmount` subtracts a value from itself and always yields 0. The discount is applied to the charge correctly but the field reporting it to the client is permanently zero, defeating the feature. Capture the original first: `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` then `response.discount_amount = originalAmount - finalAmount;`.

findings.title: FixedCouponStrategy can return a negative charge amount
findings.message: `apply` returns `amount - this.amt` with no floor. FIVEOFF (500) or TENOFF (1000) applied to a small charge (e.g. 100 cents) yields a negative amount that flows unguarded to the gateway — depending on the gateway this is an unintended credit/refund to the caller or an unhandled error, and it's exploitable as a money-extraction path. Fix: `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amt)` and reject a zero/negative finalAmount in the controller before calling the gateway.

findings.title: Empty catch silently swallows coupon errors on a payment path
findings.message: The `try/catch` around `doStuff` has an empty `// TODO` body. Any exception is dropped, `finalAmount` stays at the full price, and the charge proceeds with no log, metric, or signal to the client or to monitoring/SIEM. Decide a policy: either log and reject (422), or remove the try/catch and let the outer handler convert it to a proper error response. Don't silence failures on a money path.

findings.title: Debug console.log echoes raw user coupon code — log injection + leftover
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` is a dev leftover that writes user-supplied input verbatim to stdout. Two issues: (1) the raw string is a log-injection vector (newlines/ANSI to forge log lines, OWASP A09) and turns the log into a brute-force oracle for valid codes (valid → silent, invalid → logged); (2) the 'DEBUG' prefix shows it was never meant to ship and will spam logs on every mistyped code. Remove it, or emit a structured warn-level event / metric counter through the pino logger without embedding the raw value.

findings.title: couponCode is read from raw body and never validated by the schema
findings.message: `couponCode` is pulled off `req.body` before `chargeSchema.parse`, so it skips all validation — any type or size (null, object, 10 KB string) flows into `CouponHandlerFactory.build`. Add `couponCode: z.string().min(1).max(32).optional()` to `chargeSchema` and read it from `payload.couponCode` after parsing.

findings.title: Strategy + factory + abstract class for a 6-row lookup — YAGNI
findings.message: An abstract class (with one abstract method and no shared behaviour — effectively an interface), two strategy subclasses, and a factory exist to express six codes and two arithmetic rules, justified only by hypothetical future coupon types. Replace with a `Record<string, {kind:'percent'|'flat'; value:number}>` and a single `applyCoupon(amount, code?)` function — deletes the hierarchy and the factory. Generalise when a third coupon type actually arrives and you know the real shape needed.

findings.title: Meaningless names: doStuff, tmp, result2, x, p, amt
findings.message: `doStuff` is the exported entry point a caller reads first — rename to `applyCoupon`/`applyDiscount`. Internals: `tmp` → `strategy`, `result2` → `discountedAmount` (or return directly), `x` → `discounted`, and the private fields `p`/`amt` → `percentOff`/`fixedAmount`. Names are the cheapest documentation.

findings.title: Uses `var` and `==` where the codebase uses `const`/`let` and `===`
findings.message: Lines 16 (`var x`) and 56 (`var tmp`) use function-scoped `var`; lines 34–44 and 57 use coercing `==`. Both are footguns the rest of the codebase has opted out of. Switch to `const`/`let` and `===` (moot if the file is collapsed to a lookup table per the over-engineering finding).

findings.title: snake_case response fields in a camelCase API
findings.message: `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` are snake_case while the rest of the payload is camelCase (`customerId`, etc.). Clients reading `discountAmount`/`couponCode` will silently get `undefined`. Match the house convention: `discountAmount`, `couponCode`.

findings.title: `as any` on the charge response disables type checking on payment output
findings.message: `const response: any = transaction` turns off the compiler for the block that shapes the charge endpoint's response. Declare the real shape instead — e.g. an interface adding `discountAmount: number; couponCode?: string` to the transaction fields — so the response contract stays type-checked.

findings.title: No tests for coupon math, negative-amount, and unknown-code paths
findings.message: The PR explicitly defers tests ('Add unit tests for the coupon math (TODO follow-up)'). This is money arithmetic with two branches, six codes, a negative-amount edge, an unknown-code path, and a swallowed-error path — exactly where a silent $0 charge or negative refund hides. Coverage should land with the feature, not after.

findings.title: Delete the commented-out FREESHIP branch
findings.message: The commented `FREESHIP` branch is dead code that forces every future reader to guess whether it's a plan, a rollback, or a fossil. Git history is the backup — delete it.
